mechanism_narrative: Rising retail sales (RSAFS) signal strengthening consumer demand, which bolsters corporate revenues and drives positive S&P 500 forward returns over 20 trading days. The relationship persists across all macroeconomic environments.
failure_condition: Over any rolling 3-year window, the Spearman rank correlation between RSAFS month-over-month change and subsequent 20-day SPX return is non-positive, or the hit rate (positive returns when signal positive) is below 55%.

mechanism_narrative: A sustained decline in the unemployment rate (UNRATE) indicates labor market improvement, which lifts consumer confidence and spending, and is followed by positive S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days. The signal activates only when UNRATE falls by more than 0.5 percentage points within three months, filtering out noise.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE declines by >0.5 percentage points in 3 months
regime_hint: early recovery phase from deep recession
failure_condition: If, after a 0.5pp drop in UNRATE, the average 60-day forward return is not significantly positive (t-test p>0.05) or the signal yields a negative hit rate below 50% over a 5-year period, reject hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate is extremely low (below 1%) and retail sales are increasing, the combination of cheap financing and rising consumption creates a powerful equity tailwind. The joint signal of low rates and positive retail sales growth predicts positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days.
failure_condition: If the joint condition occurs but the subsequent 20-day SPX return is negative in more than 60% of instances, or if the average return under the condition is not significantly higher than unconditional average (one-sided t-test p>0.05), reject.

mechanism_narrative: In a high unemployment regime (UNRATE > 6%), monthly increases in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) signal a strengthening labor market and economic recovery, leading to positive S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days. In a low unemployment regime, similar payroll gains would likely be interpreted as overheating and produce negative returns, but the current regime is high unemployment.
failure_condition: If in high-unemployment periods (UNRATE > 6%) the 20-day forward return following positive PAYEMS changes is not significantly positive (one-sided t-test p>0.05), or if when UNRATE is low (<4%) the same signal produces significantly positive returns, refuting the proposed regime flip.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp monthly increase in industrial production (INDPRO) exceeding 0.5% signals a rebound in manufacturing activity, which is a leading indicator of economic expansion and boosts equity markets over a short 5-day horizon as traders react to the data release.
failure_condition: If the average 5-day forward return after an INDPRO increase >0.5% is not significantly positive (t-test p>0.05), or if the signal produces more negative-than-positive returns over any 3-year period, reject.

mechanism_narrative: When both industrial production (INDPRO) and nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) increase on a month-over-month basis simultaneously, it confirms a broad-based economic expansion that strongly supports equity market gains over the medium term of 60 trading days. The joint signal is more powerful than either in isolation.
failure_condition: If the joint condition yields an average 60-day forward return that is not positively different from the unconditional average (t-test p>0.05), or if the hit rate is below 60% over a 5-year period, reject.

mechanism_narrative: Real GDP (GDPC1) is the broadest measure of economic output. Positive quarterly growth in GDPC1 consistently maps to positive corporate earnings and stock market returns over the next 60 trading days. The relationship holds across cycles due to the fundamental link between economic output and equity valuations.
failure_condition: If the rank correlation between quarterly GDP growth and subsequent 60-day SPX return is negative over any 10-year window, or if the hit rate is below 55% in rolling tests, reject.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST) are a leading indicator of economic activity. A monthly increase of more than 10% signals a recovery in the housing market and broader economy, lifting investor sentiment and driving positive S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days. The threshold filters out noise.
failure_condition: If the average 60-day forward return after a >10% HOUST increase is not significantly positive (t-test p>0.05), or if the signal yields a win rate below 55% over a 10-year period, reject.
